data.title: GS Large Cap Equity Fund: Capitalizing On Idiosyncratic Opportunities In Q3 2025
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Goldman Sachs Large Cap Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

The Goldman Sachs Large Cap Equity Fund (GSCGX) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Index (net), during the quarter. The Information Technology and Industrials sectors contributed to returns, while the Health Care and Financials sectors detracted from returns.

AppLovin Corporation (APP) (1.4%) was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company specializes in providing services that enable mobile app developers of all sizes to market, monetize, analyze, and publish their apps through its mobile advertising, marketing, and analytics platforms. AppLovin's stock price saw positive results during the quarter due to its strong earnings results, exceeding market expectations in its revenue and earnings growth. We remain convinced that AppLovin is in the early stages of unlocking a huge new market in eCommerce. The company's stock continues to deliver excellent financial results, demonstrating strong underlying fundamentals and significant growth potential. The ongoing normalization of both the mobile app and advertising industries has potentially contributed to lifting AppLovin higher, while solid execution, operational efficiency, and strong positioning relative to peers continue to benefit the stock. We believe that the company is well-positioned to benefit from long term, secular growth trends as it leverages AI to meet the increasing demand for its programmatic mobile advertising platform, enabling mobile publishers and developers to market and monetize their applications at an accelerated pace. We remain constructive on the stock and continue to believe that AppLovin's new machine learning model is more performant than any other solution on the market, positioning the company well within this ecosystem.

D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI) (0.8%) , a homebuilding company, was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company's stock price rose on the back of its exceptionally strong financial performance and execution in its recent fiscal quarter. Despite a challenging housing market, D.R. Horton's results exceeded market expectations, underscoring its operational resilience and strategic effectiveness. We continue to believe the company may benefit from industry consolidation tailwinds and a correction of the supply/demand imbalance in the housing market. D.R. Horton's financial strength, demonstrated by robust free cash flow and share repurchases, reinforced our confidence in its ability to capitalize on the anticipated sector recovery. This optimism was further underpinned by the ongoing consolidation within the industry, where larger, more financially sound builders are increasing market share, creating a more steady and predictable competitive landscape.

Our position in manufacturer of clear dental aligning products, Align Technology, Inc. (ALGN) (0.4%) was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. The company underperformed this quarter as macroeconomic pressures weakened product demand, leading the management team to reduce future guidance. The company's clear dental aligners are highly sensitive to macroeconomic swings due to fully out of pocket costs, with traditional metal braces serving as a less-expensive trade down. As such, the company has recently experienced a weakened conversion rate of new patients becoming paying clients and extrapolated this trend to year end. Despite this headwind, we maintain long-term belief in the company as we believe the negative reaction is oversold and that the company is managing short-term pressures relatively well. Specifically, despite sales being lower, the company was able to increase margins through strategic initiatives such as innovating production through the acceleration of conversion to 3-dimensional printing. Furthermore, the company's Invisalign product is the competitive leader by a clear margin, has demonstrated significant product innovation, and has only penetrated 10% of the addressable market so far-leading us to believe there may be ample room for future growth. Ultimately we remain steadfast in our position and believe the strength of the company may overpower environmental headwinds in the long run.

Automotive and clean energy company, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) (1.1%) , was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter due to our relative underweight position in the name. The company's stock price sharply increased in September as a combination of factors fueled investor confidence. Specifically, CEO Elon Musk initiated a substantial share purchase, sending a strong signal of confidence in the company's future trajectory. Furthermore, the company reported strong vehicle deliveries, exceeding analyst expectations as expiring federal Electric Vehicle tax credits motivated customer purchases prior to their expiration at the end of the month. We continue to believe in Tesla's long-term positioning in the market given its leading position in the electric vehicle and clean energy markets. Due to the stock's volatility, premium valuation, softer demand, and lack of new product introductions in the medium-term, we continue to maintain our underweight position but believe that the company still has potential for growth as it continues to invest in artificial intelligence, autonomous driving technology, and energy storage.

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in the semiconductor equipment company, Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) (0.4%) , during the quarter. The company recently delivered an optimistic outlook for the Water Fabrication Equipment (WFE) market, fueled by significant artificial intelligence-related demand and accelerated investments in memory technologies. Management noted that new data center partnerships, particularly those related to artificial intelligence, are expected to substantially boost WFE, with Lam Research Corporation well-positioned to capture a high percentage of the high projected WFE spending over the next several years. Despite an unexpected decline in China's revenue share due to new restrictions, the company's ability to deliver strong guidance underscores its operational resilience. Overall, we initiated a position as we believe that Lam Research Corporation offers a compelling investment opportunity, driven by its critical role in the semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem, its ability to capitalize on the growing demand for advanced memory and logic driven by artificial intelligence, and its strong financial performance.

We initiated a position in investment bank and financial services company, Morgan Stanley (MS) (0.4%) , during the quarter. We believe that the company has what we believe to be a fortress balance sheet and is supported by strength in both its capital markets and wealth management verticals. Specifically, we believe that the company may benefit from utilizing its large scale and technological capabilities in the future as it continues to take market share and outperform peers, making it a potentially attractive long-term investment. Furthermore, we believe that the net interest income pipeline in its wealth management business may remain positive as the beginning of the federal interest rate cutting cycle may support further tailwinds and encourage increased lending activity. Overall, we retain a positive outlook toward the long term growth prospect of the name in an array of operating environments.

We exited our position in healthcare and insurance business, UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) (0.0%) , during the month of August. The company has endured several headwinds, including higher-than-expected care costs and an unfavorable client mix dynamic, which has pressured profitability for the company over the past several months. While we like the fundamental strength of the company, it has endured regulatory scrutiny, high utilization rates, and the tragic death of one of its executives at the company's investor event-all of which have dampened our long-term conviction in the name. Ultimately, a mid-month rally provided reprieve from this challenging environment and served as a relatively attractive point to exit our position in the name.

We exited our position in single-family home builder, Lennar Corporation (LEN) (LEN) (0.0%) , during the quarter. We began to lose conviction in the name as the spinoff of one of its verticals failed to increase its stock price and management has not proven to be as nimble as we had expected. Specifically, the company has attempted to outpace peers via competitive pricing, however, we believe this strategy may not be optimal as gross margins have become much lower. Ultimately, we believe other opportunities seem more compelling from a risk/reward standpoint, ultimately leading us to exit our position and allocate capital elsewhere.

RISK CONSIDERATIONS

data.title: Ball: Acquisitive Again, Although Small
data.description:
In September I concluded that it was time to stop kicking the can in the case of Ball Corporation (BALL). Following the divestment of the aerospace business, Ball has focused on the core aluminum can business to create more focus, although the proceeds from the divestment were quickly used to leverage up the balance sheet again after a very aggressive share buyback program.

The core aluminum can business has potential for growth, with Ball citing drivers that favor aluminum over glass and plastics to preserve and consume food and drinks. That is a long-term driver, but in reality, Ball is navigating a complex world in which consumer packaged goods volumes are pressured, tariffs have been introduced, and commodity prices are volatile.

All About Aluminum

Ball is a producer and distributor of aluminum cans, which are used in a wide range of consumer packaging applications, predominantly used to preserve food and drinks. Besides better preservation of quality and taste, aluminum has major recycling benefits as well, including lower melting temperatures and no degradation amidst its recycling process. Pre-2023, the company had a substantial aerospace business as well, activities sold in a $5.6 billion deal in 2023.

After that divestment, Ball became a $12 billion pure play on the production and sale of aluminum cans, employing about 16,000 people who are active across the globe. About half of these sales are generated in North & Central America, complemented by EMEA operations, which make up about a third of sales, with the remainder of revenues generated in South America.

Earlier this year, Ball reported its results for 2024, with sales down 2% to $11.8 billion. Adjusted earnings were reported at $3.17 per share, up twenty-seven cents from the year before. Net debt of $4.8 billion translates into a 2.5 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA reported around $2 billion, as much of the aerospace proceeds were used to buy back substantial amounts of stock.

The company reported a strong 8% increase in first quarter sales, driven by tariffs, with adjusted earnings up eight cents to $0.76 per share. Second quarter sales rose as much as 13%, with adjusted earnings up sixteen cents to $0.90 per share. At this trend and trajectory, earnings were seen around $3.50 per share as I called the composition of growth poor, driven by buybacks, which actually pushed up net debt to $6.7 billion for a leverage ratio in the mid-3s, with much of the money used for buybacks (at higher levels).

With 277 million shares trading at $49 in September, the company's equity was valued at $13.5 billion, for a $20 billion enterprise valuation. That substantial net debt load would come down a bit following the sale of a 41% stake in Ball United Arab Cab Manufacturing, with Ball obtaining $70 million in proceeds in connection with the sale.

I had some reservations about the capital allocation moves, following the aggressive buybacks in recent times, after the company actually diluted the shareholder base in the late 2010s to make acquisitions. Trading at around 10 times EBITDA, the highly leveraged business saw its equity trade at around 14 times earnings here, which is a modest multiple, yet this comes amidst an uneven track record and much debt employed (again).

Despite this, the cheapness and predictability of the business made it so that a small position could be justified, as I did not expect to update the thesis so soon, after Ball pursued M&A again, although in all honesty, it has announced a rather small deal here.

What Is Happening?

Since September, shares of Ball have been trading pretty flat at levels in the high forties. Early in November, the company reported its third-quarter results. Quarterly revenues rose by nearly 10% to $3.38 billion, aided by higher aluminum prices. Adjusted earnings of $1.02 per share were up some eleven cents on the year before, reported at $2.66 per share so far this year, making a $3.50 per share number look very attainable for the year.

Net debt was reported at $6.64 billion, for a 3.3 times leverage ratio, as net debt remained stable, despite some continued buybacks, which reduced the share count to 273 million shares, now valuing equity at $13.0 billion at $47.50 per share, for a $19.6 billion enterprise valuation. At this rate, Ball is firmly on track to generate about $13 billion in sales, with operating earnings seen around 10% of sales and earnings multiples seen in the lower to mid-teens.

With quite some debt employed and rather aggressive capital allocation decisions undertaken, I am a bit surprised to see that Ball is announcing its next deal. Early in December, Ball announced that it had reached an agreement to acquire an 80% stake in Benepack, a European can manufacturer with operations in Belgium and Hungary.

The purchase comes at a EUR 184 million payment, or about $210 million, based on prevailing currency rates. This values the transaction at about 1 percent of the current valuation, as unfortunately no financial details on the purchase have been announced.

Still Upbeat

With shares trading flat and continued progress made in the third quarter, while the next bolt-on deal is announced, I remain upbeat on the shares here given a great earnings yield, as leverage is a bit high to my taste.

The deal in Europe is really a bolt-on one but tells us little about the pro forma implications, but likely these come at reasonable valuations. Some quick searches online reveal just over $100 million in revenue contribution, as that seems largely in line, or at a small premium, to Ball's sales multiples here.

Given all this, I remain constructive here but do not expect major and spectacular developments in the business or its share price meanwhile. However, a continued set-up of solid earnings yield and continued buybacks makes the risk-reward and expected returns from here seem reasonably compelling in my view, making me continue to hold onto the shares, anticipating a partial re-rating down the road.

data.title: The 'Edge AI' Bear Case
data.description:
On “Invest Like The Best” podcast, Gavin Baker mentioned the following:

“In three years, on a bigger phone, you’ll be able to run a pruned-down version of Gemini 5, Grok 4, or ChatGPT. And that’s free. This is clearly Apple’s strategy - we’re going to make it privacy-safe and run on the phone. Other than scaling laws breaking, edge AI is by far the most plausible and scariest bear case.”

One of my hypotheses in investing in general is most bear cases never truly die; they just keep re-appearing time and again and most investors start paying more attention to these perpetual bear cases only when stock prices force them to, i.e., when stocks go down a lot. To Baker’s credit, he’s highlighting a risk when stocks are actually doing fine.

But indeed, edge AI has been touted as a plausible bear case for the “AI trade” for much of the last couple of years. Nilesh Jasani was one of the investors who actually took this bear case seriously and explained the appeal of edge AI:

“In late 2023 and early 2024, the promise of edge computing was not a niche technical idea; it was a palpable hum of excitement. The logic was clean and compelling. Moving artificial intelligence to the edge would solve the technology’s most pressing problems.First, there was privacy. Processing data on your own device meant it never had to travel to a server owned by someone else. Your secrets would remain your own. Second was latency or annoying delays. With the thinking done locally, responses would be instantaneous, a crucial feature for everything from conversations with voice assistants and augmented reality to autonomous vehicles. Finally, there was cost and access. Why rent time on a remote supercomputer when your own device could do the work? This would democratize AI, making it reliable even without a perfect internet connection.”

However, Jasani admitted that it was “wrong.” The reality didn’t match edge AI’s promises at all. From his piece again:

“Top-tier smartphones like Apple’s iPhone 16 and Samsung’s latest Galaxy look and work pretty much like the models from a few years prior. Yes, they tout AI-powered camera modes and smarter assistants, but there’s little truly new that runs independently on the device. All the major phone launches emphasized AI features, yet those features often amounted to things like slightly better autocorrect, AI image editing, or voice dictation – useful, sure, but hardly the seismic shift envisioned by edge computing evangelists.Notably, many of these “AI features” still lean on the cloud. Apple’s vaunted Apple Intelligence gave iPhones some on-device smarts, but even Apple admitted that for more “sophisticated commands” the system quietly reaches out for “help from ChatGPT” or other cloud models. In other words, your iPhone might rewrite a text message offline, but if you ask it to compose a complex email or summarize a document, it’s likely pinging a server somewhere. Apple’s commitment to privacy means those requests are anonymized and encrypted, but they’re not happening on your phone’s CPU alone.”

I recently bought an “AI PC,” and the salesperson at “Best Buy” was passionately making the case that conversations on CoPilot stays on device and not on the cloud. That’s not true. So, there is clearly a gulf of difference between what edge AI has promised so far and how much they could make it work.

But is it just a timing mismatch, and much of the promises of edge AI will eventually bear fruit? If edge AI becomes reality in the medium to long term, the biggest losers are likely to be hyperscalers who are hoping to generate billions of inference revenue on their ongoing capex spree.

Since they are Nvidia’s (NVDA) biggest customers today, you might imagine Jensen Huang to be highly incentivized to not believe in edge AI’s promises. Interestingly, Huang himself, in his recent Joe Rogan podcast appearance, made the case that edge AI is inevitable:

“the fact of the matter is your phone’s going to run AI just fine all by itself in a few years. Today, it already does it fairly decently. And so the fact that every country, every nation, every society will have the benefit of very good AI. It might not be tomorrow’s AI. It might be yesterday’s AI, but yesterday’s AI is freaking amazing. You know, in 10 years time, 9-year-old AI is going to be amazing. You don’t need 10-year old AI. You don’t need frontier AI…we need frontier AI because we want to be the world leader. But for every single country, everybody, I think the capability to elevate everybody’s knowledge and capability and intelligence, that day is coming.”

If edge AI starts to deliver on its promises, I wonder if it’s an underappreciated headwind for OpenAI (OPENAI). Following the GPT-5 backlash, Sam Altman once tweeted, “the percentage of users using reasoning models each day is significantly increasing; for example, for free users we went from <1% to 7%, and for plus users from 7% to 24%.”

It was a bit surprising to me that the vast majority of ChatGPT users are not using reasoning models. If your queries can be responded to without reasoning, those are exactly the type of queries that can be easily done by on device models in a few years.

And if the free model on your device can have private, uninterrupted (personality won’t change due to any model changes) conversations, would ChatGPT remain compelling enough for incremental users to pay for subscription?

There is indeed a real possibility that “System 1” (fast/reflexive) queries are just commodities and it is the “System 2” (deep thinking) queries that prove to be high-value ones. It may be instructive to think about why Anthropic (ANTHRO) is almost solely focusing on “System 2” queries, whereas ChatGPT’s vast majority of queries are still likely to be “System 1” queries.

System 1 queries can be quite profitable (very low inference costs now, which may even be almost zero in an edge AI scenario), but it is also going to be very hard to convince users that it is worth paying a subscription for in a few years.

Of course, we cannot quite outline the impact of certain risks playing out, imagining ceteris paribus. Sam Altman has already declared “code red” in the face of Gemini’s recent growth spurt, and OpenAI will certainly not sit idly if edge AI ever starts proving to be a headwind for user growth.

Let’s not forget that OpenAI still has the “Midas touch” when it comes to its product. They may be behind on model quality, they may be less focused than Anthropic, and they clearly don’t have the infrastructure advantage that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) does, but AI still is synonymous with “ChatGPT” for most people. That itself will likely buy them time, but the clock is ticking.
